title: 8 Nehase — St. Eleutherius & St. Leonidas & The Assembly
narrative:
Nehase

St. Eleutherius the Martyr

St. Leonidas of Alexandria, Father of Origen

St. Myron of Crete, Bishop and Martyr

St. Baulus the Martyr

The eighth day of Nehase, the second day of the Filseta fast, assembles a company of witnesses bound together by the thread of holy suffering accepted in freedom. St. Eleutherius — the free one — and St. Leonidas, the father of the great Alexandrian theologian Origen, stand at the head of today's commemoration as examples of that most paradoxical of Christian truths: that the deepest freedom is found precisely in the willingness to die. The faithful who are now fasting in honor of the Virgin are practicing a small version of this same mystery — freely choosing constraint in order to discover liberty, freely embracing hunger in order to be fed from a higher table.

St. Leonidas of Alexandria was beheaded in the Severan persecution of 202 A.D., leaving behind his son Origen — then seventeen years old — who would have rushed to martyrdom himself had his mother not hidden his clothes, preventing him from leaving the house. Origen later wrote that he had urged his father by letter not to draw back from martyrdom for the sake of his family's welfare. What kind of formation produces a seventeen-year-old who urges his father toward death? The formation of a household so saturated with the Gospel that its members have genuinely learned to love God more than life. Leonidas is thus not only a martyr but the head of a domestic church that produced one of the greatest and most controversial theological minds in Christian history — a reminder that the domestic church is a real church, with real apostolic power.

St. Myron of Crete served as a bishop beloved for his gentleness and his care for the poor, who was seized during a pagan festival and put to death when he refused to participate in idol worship. His example speaks to the Filseta community: the fast is not only an interior discipline but an external witness. To fast visibly, to abstain publicly, to pray when the surrounding world is feasting, is a form of the same witness that Myron made in the public square of Crete. The Christian who keeps Filseta is, in a small but real way, doing what Myron did: standing in the public space of daily life and saying, by the witness of the body, 'There is something more important than this.'

St. Baulus completes today's assembly as one of the anonymous-but-beloved martyrs who fill the deep calendar of the Church with countless names that scholarship cannot fully illuminate but faith fully receives. The Ethiopian Synaxarium has always had space for those whose detailed histories have not survived — because the Church knows that God's memory is more complete than any archive, and that the blood of every martyr, however obscure, has watered the same garden. As Filseta deepens on this eighth day, the Church carries all of them — Eleutherius, Leonidas, Myron, Baulus — as luminous company on the ascent toward the great feast.
